Home / Glossary / Predictive Analytics
Glossary

What is predictive analytics?

Updated 2026-04-15

Predictive analytics is the practice of using historical data, statistics, and machine learning to forecast future outcomes such as next-quarter revenue, customer churn, equipment failure, or fraud risk. It sits between descriptive analytics (what happened) and prescriptive analytics (what to do about it).

$22.1B → $67.7B
Global predictive-analytics market projected growth between 2023 and 2028, a 25%+ compound annual growth rate.
MarketsandMarkets — Predictive Analytics Market 2023
5–25%
Typical revenue lift retailers report from churn-prediction-driven retention campaigns vs. blanket campaigns.
Bain & Company — The Value of Online Customer Loyalty

Common model families

  • Linear regression — interpretable, fast, baseline.
  • Logistic regression — yes/no outcomes (churn, fraud).
  • Tree ensembles — Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM. Strongest default for tabular data.
  • Time-series — ARIMA, Prophet, ETS for forecasting with trend and seasonality.
  • Neural networks — when you have lots of data and weak feature engineering.

How to know it's working

Pick a metric that matches the business cost: RMSE for forecasts, AUC for ranking, recall@k for fraud, MAPE for finance. A predictive model that improves a metric on a holdout set but never moves a business KPI is a science-fair project, not analytics.

Frequently asked questions

Do I need a data scientist?

Not for the common patterns. DataVision Pro's predictions tab covers linear models and tree-based forecasting in a few clicks.

How much history do I need?

For seasonal forecasts, at least two full cycles (e.g. 24 months for monthly seasonality). For classification, at least a few hundred examples per class.

What's the biggest pitfall?

Target leakage — accidentally using a feature that is only known after the outcome occurred. It produces unrealistically good test scores and miserable production performance.

How is it different from forecasting?

Forecasting is a subset focused on time-series outputs. Predictive analytics also covers classification, ranking, and risk scoring.

How do you validate a predictive model?

Time-aware holdouts (train on past, test on future), cross-validation, and — crucially — monitoring the live metric after deployment.

Related terms

K-Means Clustering

An unsupervised algorithm that groups observations into K clusters by minimising within-cluster variance.

Data Drift

A change over time in the statistical properties of input data that degrades a model's accuracy.

Outlier Detection

Statistical techniques for finding observations that lie an abnormal distance from the rest of a dataset.

Missing Value Imputation

Filling in missing data with estimated values so downstream analysis and models can run without dropping rows.
